prompt:
Create a therapeutic audio session for the following goal. Write the full script in English, approximately 10 minutes long when read aloud.

## Goal
Title: Reduce bedtime anxiety and fall asleep independently
Description: Help Noah manage fear at bedtime and build confidence sleeping alone.

## Person
This is for Noah (age 7).
Developmental Tier: MIDDLE_CHILDHOOD

## Therapeutic Focus
Bedtime Fear and Sleep Avoidance
Category: BEHAVIORAL
Description: Noah becomes anxious at bedtime, needing a parent to stay until he falls asleep.

## Recommendations
- Predictable bedtime routine
- Gradual fading of parent presence
- Comfort object use

## Sparkling Moments
- 2024-03-01: Stayed in his room alone for 10 minutes without calling out
- 2024-03-08: Fell asleep before a parent checked on him for the first time

## Research Evidence
The following research papers inform the therapeutic techniques to use:

1. "Graduated extinction and parental fading for pediatric sleep problems" (2023)
   Key findings: Gradual parental fading reduced sleep-onset latency by 40%; gains maintained at 6 months
   Therapeutic techniques: Parental fading; bedtime pass technique; positive reinforcement of brave behavior

2. "CBT for childhood nighttime fears: A meta-analysis" (2022)
   Key findings: CBT reduced nighttime anxiety in 7-12 year olds; imagery rescripting was most effective technique
   Therapeutic techniques: Imagery rescripting; thought challenging; relaxation training; rewards for brave nights

## Instructions
- Create a complete, flowing therapeutic audio script
- Incorporate specific techniques and findings from the research above
- When clinical notes are available, weave their insights and observations into the session
- Personalize for Noah (age 7) (developmental tier: MIDDLE_CHILDHOOD)
- Target duration: 10 minutes when read aloud at a calm pace
- Write in English
- Follow the therapeutic audio content structure (warm introduction, understanding the challenge, guided practices, integration)
- Include a brief mention that a parent, caregiver, or professional can provide additional support if needed
- IMPORTANT: Do NOT use any markdown formatting (no **, ##, *, bullet points, or bold/italic syntax). Write plain spoken prose only, as the script will be read aloud by a text-to-speech engine
script:
Welcome, Noah. I'm so glad you're here, ready for a special bedtime adventure. I know sometimes bedtime can feel a little scary, and that's okay. Lots of kids feel that way. Tonight, we're going to practice being the boss of those bedtime worries and build up your superpower of falling asleep all by yourself. You've already done some really brave things, like staying in your room alone and even falling asleep before a check-in. We're going to use that bravery. Get cozy in your bed, maybe hug your favorite stuffed animal or blanket, and let's begin.

First, let's understand what's happening. When it's dark and quiet, our brains sometimes like to play tricks on us, showing us scary pictures or telling us worried stories. It's not because anything is really wrong; it's just your imagination being very, very strong. And that's actually a good thing, because it means you have a powerful imagination. We can use that same power to imagine good, safe, and happy things instead. The goal is for you to feel so safe and cozy in your own room that you can drift off to sleep feeling proud and strong, knowing you did it.

Now, let's start with making our bodies feel super calm and ready for sleep. This is called relaxation training. Put one hand on your tummy. Let's take a slow, deep breath in through your nose, like you're smelling a warm cookie. Feel your hand rise. And now breathe out slowly through your mouth, like you're blowing out birthday candles. Let's do that two more times. Breathe in the calm... and blow out the worry. Good. One more. Breathe in the cozy feeling... and blow out the fidgets. Excellent.

Next, we're going to use your amazing imagination for something really cool. This is called imagery rescripting. We're going to rewrite the scary movie in your mind and change it into a happy one. Close your eyes if you want to. Think of a place that makes you feel one hundred percent safe and happy. Maybe it's building a giant pillow fort in your living room, or being at the park on a sunny day, or cuddling with your pet. Picture it in your mind. What do you see? Are there bright colors? What do you hear? Maybe happy, quiet sounds. What do you feel? Probably warm and smiley. Now, imagine your bed is right in the middle of that happy place. You are lying in your bed, but you're surrounded by this total happiness. Your brave stuffed animal is right there with you, guarding the fort. This is your safe, sleepy scene. You can visit this place anytime you feel a worry at bedtime.

Now, let's practice a special trick for being brave. It's called the bedtime pass technique. In your mind, I want you to picture a special ticket, like a golden ticket. This is your bravery pass. It means you are officially a brave sleeper. When you have a worry, you can think about your pass and remember, I am practicing. I am brave. I can use my calm breathing and my happy place. And every night you stay in your bed and practice, you earn a point for being brave. Maybe you and your grown-up can talk about a fun reward for collecting those bravery points, like a special story or activity.

Let's put it all together for a final practice. Get comfortable. Take one more deep breath in... and a long breath out. Now, bring back your happy, safe place in your mind. You are there, in your cozy bed. Your guard stuffed animal is on duty. You are holding your golden bravery pass. Your body feels heavy and relaxed. If a worry tries to visit, just notice it, and then gently go back to your safe scene. You are the director of this movie, and you choose the happy ending. With every breath, you feel sleepier, and safer, and prouder.

You did an amazing job tonight, Noah. Remember, being brave at bedtime is like building a muscle—it gets stronger every time you practice. Use your calm breathing. Visit your happy place. Remember your bravery pass. And know that you have already done this—staying in your room and falling asleep on your own. You can do it again. Your grown-ups are so proud of you, and they are always nearby if you really need them. Keep being the brave sleep explorer that you are. Sweet dreams, superhero.
